posts.name: Alex Rocha
posts.cooked:
Hey all! I received a '18 DRC Romanee St. Vivant as a gift. I haven’t really had any Burgundy close to DRC level but have read that a lot of people are not fans of the St. Vivant. I’m considering selling it here or through K&L, or waiting and drinking it.
Would you sell it and get several other burgs or would you wait and drink it?
If I do end up holding it, any suggestions for how long?
Thanks!

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
AlexR0cha:

Hey all! I received a '18 DRC Romanee St. Vivant as a gift. I haven’t really had any Burgundy close to DRC level but have read that a lot of people are not fans of the St. Vivant. I’m considering selling it here or through K&L, or waiting and drinking it.
Would you sell it and get several other burgs or would you wait and drink it?
If I do end up holding it, any suggestions for how long?
Thanks!

I think recently the RSV has gotten a lot better. The 2018 is actually really good although at this point in its life cycle likely needs a bit more time in the cellar before its drinking at its best. I imagine if you were to sell it you’d probably get somewhere around $2k for the bottle, so you’d have to decide whether it’s worth drinking.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked: Since you have never had DRC, drink it. It is approachable now. It will of course get better with age.

posts.name: Alex Rocha
posts.cooked: Drink it now is the sort of advice that I come here for

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked: You only live once and DRC is a unique experience.

posts.name: Alex Rocha
posts.cooked: I was thinking it’s worth waiting 2-4 years. Do you think the gains are marginal?

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: Yes I think the gains are marginal. 20 years—that’s a different story.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: I suspect that it will be quite markedly less attractive in 2-4 years than it will be now, but one can only ever guess at these things.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: If you’re going to open it, I’d do it now.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Not a fan of the RSV compared to what? Compared to Leroy? The DRC RSV is typically charming and lovely. The 18 is not going to be the best example of what DRC is best at.

posts.name: Billy Norris
posts.cooked: This is the correct answer.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: If you want to drink tasty pinot, have it now. If you want to potentially experience what DRC is about, wait 10 years, at least.

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked: My limited expedrience with DRC RSV is that it’s very fine. I have a few bottles and I am waiting on mine, but it depends on whether you like fruit forward wine or prefer aged notes and potentially greater complexity in your wine.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: The correct answer is buy 2 more and drink one now, one in 2034 and one in 2044.

posts.name: M. Ch@o
posts.cooked: Sell and buy La Tache

posts.name: Alex Rocha
posts.cooked: I would if I had an extra $1,000 laying around

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: I’m going to give contrarian advice, sorry. Unless drinking DRC is somehow important to you, sell it and buy multiple bottles of something you’re likely to love. A 2018 now seems like a complete waste to me. It won’t “reveal” to you what’s special about DRC.

Rauno_E_NZ:

I’m going to give contrarian advice, sorry. Unless drinking DRC is somehow important to you, sell it and buy multiple bottles of something you’re likely to love. A 2018 now seems like a complete waste to me. It won’t “reveal” to you what’s special about DRC.

Agree with Rauno - my best DRC experiences were with wines at least 20, 25+ years old, better 35-40 -
young bottles were at best interesting, even impressive, but far from magical.
1980 Rb, 1978 LT, 1971 GE, 1979 RC …

If you won’t wait better sell it.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Yes - very good and very sound advice by and from Paul and Rauno…

Burgundy is not ( repeat : not ) just for a few famous plots and a few famous producers.

They are wines for many occasions and very personal. It is your own journey with experience that counts …

Here is a Chinese Poem :

Here is the whole text : :

The rains and the clouds in the Loa’s mountains and the tide of Qiantang river;
They are thousand of miles away and you do not wish not to be there.
You were there and returned sound and save;
The rains and the clouds in the Loa’s mountains and the tide of Qiantang river,

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked: My humble opinion? If you’ve got only one bottle of that famous domaine’s wines, then keep it for yourself and share it with someone dear to you. Why selling? You only live once…Like my good friend Peter Chiu wisely states : there are bottles for many occasions. Surely a grand occasion will be needed for this bottle but I have no doubts you will find one. Me myself had great experiences about 5 years ago having a go at 2001 and 99 LA TACHE but those more familiar with DRC and its wines confirm that the R-st-Vivant has become a household name too.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
I’m going to go ahead and disagree with the other posters.

I’ve had a lot of DRC from a variety of ages, including that bottling very recently, and it showed very well.

I don’t think if you have one bottle “cellar it for 20 years” is very good advice. If they had a case, then sure at least some of the bottles should be drank after significant aging.

While it’s preferable to experience the wines during a range of maturities, that’s not really what the options are. I’d either sell/trade it, or drink it immediately.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: I love the RSV. It tends to be more open and pleasurable. That being said if you pop it now you will get a bunch of primary fruit. I would hold for 20 years if time is in your side.

posts.name: Richard Albert
posts.cooked:
Went to the DRC website just now and noticed the earliest tasting notes listed are from 2013.
Curious, no?
Are they making a statement about when to drink?

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: I had the '70 not that long ago. It was superb. And today’s winemaking is far superior. There is a loooooooooong runway on these.

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked: Just had the 2012 and 2020 side by side…the 2020 was shut down hard, the 2012 magnificent! You got it as a gift, have nothing to lose…go grab a burger with a buddy, bring another bottle to pair, and ENJOY!

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
Rauno_E_NZ:

sell it and buy multiple bottles of something you’re likely to love.

agree completely.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: If an extra $4k in your pocket would make a difference in your life you should sell it. (If you’d gotten $4k as a gift instead, you wouldn’t spend it on a bottle of DRC, would you?) If the $4k doesn’t register, I would follow Michael’s advice and drink it and enjoy the experience. Who knows where you’ll be in 20 years? Maybe (hopefully) by that point you’ll have had lots more opportunities to try DRC or other epic wines and it won’t be the special, unique experience then that it would be for you now. So take the opportunity you’ve got now. That’s assuming Burgundy is your preferred poison. Otherwise sell it or swap it for a 6-pack of Lafite. Or a bottle of Lafite and a wad of cash. (FWIW, I do agree RSV is sometimes an underperformer in the DRC stable and if I were going to drop that kind of coin or opportunity cost on a DRC I’d rather do it with Richebourg.)

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
am i the only person interested in the circumstances surrounding this amazing gift?

OP can you share the story?
Sell and buy Champagne!

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Let me just to do a summary …here.

Everyone is right as it is quite personal in enjoyments of wine.

For example price-wise for DRC RStV and DRC-Rich is in the same range. So Keith is right in that he would to prefer to do it with Richebourg.

For me the key key is : Alex drinks other burgundy wines too. So here is my suggestion : talk to the KL guy and ask him to sell it and buy many bottles of your preferred red burgundy from vintage 2022.

posts.name: Dav1d S@wyer
posts.cooked:
I would drink it since it’s a gift.You’ll have the memory. In my opinion, trophy wines like this are as much about the experience as anything (or at least that’s how I justify opening mine).

The Domaine is high on 2018 and RSV is only getting better. FWIW I opened an '08 earlier this year and it was gorgeous.

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked: Sell it and buy 2 cases of Hudelot Bourgogne Rouge and a few bottles of their 1ers. Drink beer with the change.

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked: Gift bottle is a chance to pretend that it’s like the 1990s again when you could just go down to the LWS, buy a bottle for not much more than a hundred bucks, and enjoy it with a good steak on a whim. Some vintages didn’t drink so great on release but a lot of these were just great, if not developed, and all indications are this one is drinking fine. Do it, definitely, especially since you seem to be a fan of red Burgs.

posts.name: Brent Clayton

Drink it. Either now or later. Don’t sell/flip/trade it.

My first DRC was 1990 RSV and I split it with the fellas at work so we all had a glass. $125. It was hard as nails. Should’ve bought it for myself and kept it.

posts.name: Todd Tucker
posts.cooked:
Blockquote Hold the phone. We’ve spent all this time debating whether to open it or sell it, and now you tell us it was a personal birthday gift from your Dad ? If he wanted to give you money, he would have…

Blockquote

I’d have to agree with this one. It’s a gift. Drink it, and I’m on team “drink it later when it will separate itself from the pack”. If I had gifted one my children a bottle of DRC, which will most certainly happen at some point when they are of age, and they sold it, I would likely not gift them a similar bottle again. It would not be to punish them, but probably in realizing that that is what I wanted to give them rather than what they needed or wanted. Next time around, I would likely give them money or repair a car, etc if that’s what they needed.

Now when I die and I leave them my cellar, then they may do with it as they wish. I think that’s different.

He probably did this out of love and a sense of connection of a mutual passion (assuming he also drinks wine) gifting a special bottle for a special day for you knowing you likely would not ever buy one on your own. A bottle of DRC is a special, very thoughtful, and incredibly generous gift. I know it’s easy to say money counts more in certain situations, I am just not so sure this is one of them.

reply_to_user.name: Tom G l a s g o w

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
MChang:

I’m going to go ahead and disagree with the other posters.

I’ve had a lot of DRC from a variety of ages, including that bottling very recently, and it showed very well.

I don’t think if you have one bottle “cellar it for 20 years” is very good advice. If they had a case, then sure at least some of the bottles should be drank after significant aging.

While it’s preferable to experience the wines during a range of maturities, that’s not really what the options are. I’d either sell/trade it, or drink it immediately.

Absolutely spot on IMHO. I have not had much recent DRC, but to this day I have more TNs for DRC than any other winery- and RSV has always been my favorite. It does shut down to an extent in most vintages, but generally speaking I find it approachable after a decade- a bit less for lighter years like 92 and 00 and a bit longer for unusually robust years like 93 and 99.

When I started collecting wine, DRC at auction actually started to go down in value once it was 20-30 years old unless it was a rare ancient bottle. A good friend of mine in my original tasting group in college (I was in college- he was a retired software engineer) used to bring lots of DRC to tastings. One night he brought a 1959 Grands Echezeaux that was pure magic. I asked him why he had never brought a 78 or 85 and he told me he had consumed them long before when they were at their best. I last had the famed 1980 La Tache in 1998 and it was just passing peak. Back then it was I think around $500 a bottle. These days it is well into the five figures a bottle and surely just a shell of its former self.

Until Rudy came along and gave a lot of people an inaccurate impression of how long burgundies take to mature- the above snippets are prime examples of conventional wisdom. Some people do prefer the wines after decades, and they will rarely be disappointed- but you do not have to wait 20 years for most DRCs and certainly not for RSV in my opinion. And I think most people would be let down waiting that long.

That said, I would either hold this to 2030 or drink it very soon before it goes into its little dumb phase.

RSV is a wine of aromatics. Only Romanee-Conti itself is more complex and RSV has a profile very much like RC, though without that incredble spherical perfection, where La Tache has all sorts of wonderful sauvage notes. In a good vintage, the palate will have a gorgeous core of glowing mid-weight deft fruit akin to a fine old Soldera or Ausone, but the aromatics are what really set it apart.

And so your best bet is simple fare- maybe a steak or piece of game, perhaps with a berry-based or Bordelais sauce, but avoid creamy sauces. And pick a time when you can slowly savor it over several hours as it develops.

One final thought on the decision of whether to drink or sell- one good argument for drinking, especially if you know that it was purchased at release and well stored, is provenance.

DRC trades very heavily these days, and it is not unrealistic to assume that a bottle might change hands 5-10 or more times in just a few years. It may be well stored in between, but all that transporting about greatly increases the risk of 1-2 days of mild heat exposure that might leave the wine performing at 90% capacity, but at this level that lost 10% (in the form of lost freshness and vibrancy mostly) makes all the difference in the world.

If you would like to try a DRC and a trustworthy bottle comes your way- drink it. The logistics and risks of getting another one later in the secondary market are significant.

Thanks for the insights everyone, looks like I’m going to be drinking this one.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Don’t coravin it.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Aubert is actually quite explicit in saying that the wines can drink very well young. He often cites the 1969 de Vogüé Musigny, which he drank a bunch of in the early 1970s, as never having tasted better than out of the gates. And I think in riper, much higher pH vintages than we used to have in the past, that’s more likely to occur than it was in the 1980s or '90s. So in short, I think the website isn’t sending any kind of implicit message, it just hasn’t been updated for a while.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked: my advice only changes slightly! drink it now with a great pizza!

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked: I have had a lot of recent vintage DRCs and agree they drink well young and you can see the magic.

posts.name: Clay Adkinson
posts.cooked: RSV and Corton actually drink very well young imo.
